questions.question:
Choose the appropriate word/phrase, out of the options given below, to complete the following sentence:

Dhoni, as well as the other members of Indian team, _________ present on the occasion.

A. were  B. was  C. has  D. have
questions.to_find: correct verb form to complete the sentence following subject-verb agreement

understand.plain: When a subject is joined to additional nouns by 'as well as', the verb agrees with the main subject only, not the intervening phrase. Here 'Dhoni' is the true subject and is singular, so the verb must also be singular. 'As well as the other members' is a parenthetical phrase and does not affect the verb form.

keywords.explain: The verb must match the number (singular/plural) of the true subject of the sentence.
keywords.example: The teacher, as well as the students, was present. (singular verb)

keywords.explain: A phrase introduced by 'as well as', 'along with', 'together with', or 'besides' is parenthetical and does not change the number of the main subject.
keywords.example: The manager, along with her team, is travelling.

understand.visual_alt: A sentence diagram showing the main subject 'Dhoni' controlling verb agreement despite the phrase 'as well as the other members' being present.

given.aim: Select the correct verb form to complete the sentence about Dhoni and the Indian team.

terms.meaning: The primary noun that controls verb agreement
terms.example: Dhoni is the main subject here
terms.connects: determines singular or plural verb

terms.meaning: A conjunction-like phrase that introduces additional, but non-controlling, subjects
terms.example: Sachin, as well as his teammates, was felicitated.
terms.connects: does not change verb number

terms.meaning: The third-person singular form of a verb (e.g., was, is, has)
terms.example: He was present. She is here.
terms.connects: required when main subject is singular

given.plan: Identify the true subject 'Dhoni' (singular), note that 'as well as the other members' is parenthetical, and pick the singular past-tense verb 'was'.

steps.title: Identify the main subject
steps.apply: The sentence begins with 'Dhoni' — a singular proper noun. The phrase 'as well as the other members of Indian team' is set off as a parenthetical addition and does NOT become part of the compound subject.
steps.note: Phrases introduced by 'as well as', 'along with', 'in addition to' are parenthetical — they do not make the subject plural.

steps.title: Choose the verb that agrees with the singular subject
steps.apply: Since 'Dhoni' is singular, the verb must be singular past tense: 'was'. Option A ('were') is plural, C ('has') implies present perfect (wrong tense context), and D ('have') is plural present — all incorrect.
steps.note: 'Was' is the correct singular past-tense form.

questions.formula_note: Subject-verb agreement rule: when 'as well as' introduces an additional noun, the verb agrees only with the primary subject.

questions.question:
Choose the correct verb to fill in the blank below:

Let us ___________

A. introvert  B. alternate  C. atheist  D. altruist
questions.to_find: the word that is a verb suitable for filling the blank

understand.plain: Among the four options, only one is a verb in the context given. 'Introvert', 'atheist', and 'altruist' are nouns (or occasionally adjectives), while 'alternate' can function as a verb meaning to take turns or vary. The sentence 'Let us alternate' is grammatically correct.

keywords.explain: A word that expresses an action, occurrence, or state of being.
keywords.example: alternate — to switch between two things in turns

keywords.explain: To occur or cause to occur in turn repeatedly; to switch back and forth.
keywords.example: Let us alternate between the two strategies.

understand.visual_alt: A table listing each option word with its part of speech, highlighting 'alternate' as the only verb.

given.aim: Identify the option word that serves as a verb to complete 'Let us ___________'.

terms.meaning: A noun/adjective describing a shy, inward-focused person; not typically used as a standalone verb in formal grammar
terms.example: She is an introvert.
terms.connects: Not grammatically appropriate here

terms.meaning: A noun meaning a person who does not believe in god
terms.example: He is an atheist.
terms.connects: Cannot serve as a verb

terms.meaning: A noun meaning a person who is unselfish and concerned with others' welfare
terms.example: She is a true altruist.
terms.connects: Cannot serve as a verb

given.plan: Eliminate words that are only nouns or adjectives; the one word that works as a verb after 'Let us' is the answer.

steps.title: Test each option as a verb after 'Let us'
steps.apply: A. 'Let us introvert' — not standard (introvert is typically a noun/adjective). C. 'Let us atheist' — grammatically wrong (atheist is a noun). D. 'Let us altruist' — grammatically wrong (altruist is a noun). B. 'Let us alternate' — correct (alternate is a verb meaning to take turns).
steps.note: 'Alternate' as a verb: to switch repeatedly between two things.

steps.title: Confirm 'alternate' is a verb
steps.apply: 'Alternate' has multiple forms: alternate (verb), alternation (noun), alternating (adjective/participle). 'Let us alternate' is a standard imperative construction.
steps.note: The question specifically asks for a verb — only 'alternate' qualifies.

questions.formula_note: Part-of-speech identification: only the option that functions as a verb is grammatically correct in the 'Let us ___' construction.

questions.question:
Choose the appropriate word/phrase, out of the options given below, to complete the following sentence:

Apparent lifelessness _________ dormant life.

A. harbours  B. leads to  C. supports  D. affects
questions.to_find: the appropriate word that correctly describes how apparent lifelessness conceals dormant life

understand.plain: The sentence states that something appearing lifeless actually contains or conceals dormant (inactive but living) life. The word 'harbour' means to shelter or keep hidden, which fits the idea that lifeless-looking objects may secretly contain life that is simply dormant. 'Leads to', 'supports', and 'affects' do not carry this nuance.

keywords.explain: To hold or contain within; to provide shelter for something concealed.
keywords.example: The old building harbours many secrets.

keywords.explain: Temporarily inactive but capable of becoming active; in a state of rest.
keywords.example: Seeds can remain dormant for decades before germinating.

understand.visual_alt: A diagram showing a seemingly lifeless desert surface above which lies hidden underground life, illustrating 'harbours dormant life'.

given.aim: Find the word that best expresses the relationship between apparent lifelessness and dormant life.

terms.meaning: The outward appearance of having no life, even though life may actually be present
terms.example: A dry tundra in winter appears lifeless.
terms.connects: subject of the sentence

terms.meaning: Life that exists in a state of inactivity, ready to resume at a later stage
terms.example: Spores can sustain dormant life for centuries.
terms.connects: the hidden reality being described

terms.meaning: Conceals, shelters, or contains within
terms.example: Still water harbours danger.
terms.connects: the verb that links apparent lifelessness to hidden life

given.plan: Select the verb that means 'contains hidden within' — connecting the outer appearance of lifelessness to the inner reality of dormant life.

steps.title: Analyse the semantic relationship
steps.apply: The sentence implies a contrast: something looks lifeless but actually contains life. The verb must bridge this contrast by expressing concealment or containment. 'Harbours' (A) perfectly conveys 'contains within itself, albeit hidden'. 'Leads to' (B) suggests causality — lifelessness causing dormant life, which is illogical. 'Supports' (C) implies active sustenance. 'Affects' (D) is too vague and neutral.
steps.note: 'Harbour' in biology contexts means 'to be the host or shelter of'.

steps.title: Confirm by substitution
steps.apply: Substituting: 'Apparent lifelessness harbours dormant life.' This reads naturally — a lifeless-looking environment is actually hosting dormant organisms. The other options produce awkward or incorrect meanings.
steps.note: This type of question tests contextual vocabulary — the exact meaning of each option word.

questions.formula_note: Contextual word-choice: 'harbour' uniquely captures the idea of hidden containment that bridges apparent lifelessness with dormant life.

questions.question:
Fill in the blank with the correct idiom/phrase:

That boy from the town was a _________ in the sleepy village.

A. dog out of herd  B. cat out of water  C. fish out of water  D. bird from the flock
questions.to_find: the idiom that means someone is uncomfortable or out of place in an unfamiliar environment

understand.plain: The sentence describes someone (a town boy) who is in an unfamiliar setting (a sleepy village) and feels out of place. The idiom 'fish out of water' perfectly captures this — a fish removed from water is completely out of its natural environment. The other options are not standard English idioms.

keywords.explain: An idiom describing someone who is in an unfamiliar or uncomfortable situation, far from their natural environment.
keywords.example: As a city person at a rural festival, she felt like a fish out of water.

keywords.explain: A phrase whose meaning is not deducible from the literal meanings of its individual words.
keywords.example: 'Break the ice' means to relieve tension, not to physically break ice.

understand.visual_alt: A fish drawn on dry land, representing someone out of their natural element.

given.aim: Identify the standard English idiom that describes a person who is out of place in an unfamiliar environment.

terms.meaning: A quiet, slow-paced rural settlement — contrasting with a busy town
terms.example: He moved from Mumbai to a sleepy village in the hills.
terms.connects: the unfamiliar setting for the town boy

terms.meaning: A person in an unfamiliar or uncomfortable situation
terms.example: The engineer at a poetry recital was a fish out of water.
terms.connects: the standard idiom that fits the context

terms.meaning: Options A, B, D are not standard English idioms
terms.example: 'Dog out of herd' and 'cat out of water' are not conventional expressions.
terms.connects: used to test knowledge of exact idiomatic phrases

given.plan: Recognise that only 'fish out of water' is a legitimate English idiom conveying the meaning needed in the sentence.

steps.title: Understand the context
steps.apply: A 'town boy' in a 'sleepy village' is in an unfamiliar environment — the sentence calls for an idiom meaning 'out of place'. The idiom 'fish out of water' precisely conveys this feeling of displacement.
steps.note: Other options (A, B, D) are not established English idioms and can be eliminated.

steps.title: Verify the correct idiom
steps.apply: 'Fish out of water' is a very common English idiom. The substituted sentence reads: 'That boy from the town was a fish out of water in the sleepy village.' — clear and natural English.
steps.note: Always verify idiom questions by checking whether the phrase is a recognised expression.

questions.formula_note: Idiom recognition: 'fish out of water' is the only standard English idiom among the options that fits the context of displacement.

questions.question:
If I were you, I _________ that laptop. It's much too expensive.

A. Shan't buy  B. Shall buy  C. Wouldn't buy  D. Would buy
questions.to_find: the correct conditional verb form expressing unwillingness to buy

understand.plain: This is a second conditional sentence ('If I were you...' — an unreal present condition). The second conditional uses 'would/could/might + base verb' in the result clause. Since the speaker is advising against buying the laptop ('It's much too expensive'), the negative form 'wouldn't buy' is the correct choice. 'Would buy' is positive (wrong intent), and 'shan't/shall buy' belong to first conditionals about real future events.

keywords.explain: A hypothetical or unreal condition about the present/future: 'If + past tense, would + base verb.'
keywords.example: If I were a bird, I would fly home.

keywords.explain: Contraction of 'would not' — used for polite refusal or hypothetical negative action.
keywords.example: I wouldn't do that if I were you.

understand.visual_alt: A conditional diagram: 'If I were you' (unreal condition) leads to the result 'I wouldn't buy that laptop' (hypothetical negative action).

given.aim: Choose the correct modal verb phrase that expresses not buying the expensive laptop in a second conditional structure.

terms.meaning: A fixed expression for giving advice using the second conditional; 'were' is the subjunctive form
terms.example: If I were you, I would save more money.
terms.connects: sets up the hypothetical condition

terms.meaning: Would not buy — expresses hypothetical negative intent
terms.example: I wouldn't buy a car I can't afford.
terms.connects: matches the negative advisory tone of the sentence

terms.meaning: Extremely expensive; far beyond a reasonable price
terms.example: That phone is much too expensive for a student.
terms.connects: justification for the negative choice — not buying

given.plan: Identify the second conditional structure, note the advisory/negative intent ('too expensive'), and select 'wouldn't buy' as the appropriate form.

steps.title: Identify the conditional type
steps.apply: 'If I were you' uses the subjunctive past tense ('were'), marking this as a second conditional (hypothetical / unreal). The result clause must take the form 'would + base verb'.
steps.note: Second conditional: If + past subjunctive, would/wouldn't + infinitive.

steps.title: Apply the negative intent
steps.apply: The follow-up clause 'It's much too expensive' signals that the speaker recommends NOT buying. So the result clause must be negative: 'wouldn't buy'. Options A (shan't buy) and B (shall buy) use first-conditional forms. D (would buy) is positive and contradicts the sentiment.
steps.note: 'Shan't' is a first-conditional form and extremely archaic; 'wouldn't' is the natural second-conditional negative.

questions.formula_note: Second conditional rule: If + past subjunctive, would/wouldn't + base verb; the negative form matches the advisory tone of the sentence.

questions.question:
Choose the most appropriate set of words from the options given below to complete the following sentence:

___________ is a will, ___________ is a way.

A. Were, their, their  B. Were, there, there  C. Where, there, there  D. Where, their, their
questions.to_find: the correct words to complete the proverb 'Where there is a will, there is a way'

understand.plain: The sentence is a well-known English proverb: 'Where there is a will, there is a way.' It means that if someone is determined to do something, they will find a means to achieve it. The blanks require 'Where' (an adverb of place introducing the condition) and 'there' (an expletive/adverb) — not 'were' (past tense of be) or 'their' (possessive pronoun).

keywords.explain: A short, well-known saying that expresses a general truth or piece of advice.
keywords.example: 'Where there is a will, there is a way' encourages determination.

keywords.explain: 'Where' is an adverb of place; 'were' is the past plural/subjunctive of 'be'. They are homophones in informal speech but completely different words.
keywords.example: Where is the key? (adverb) vs They were late. (verb)

understand.visual_alt: Two blanks labelled: first blank = 'Where' (adverb of place), second blank = 'there' (expletive adverb), completing the classic proverb.

given.aim: Identify the correct pair of words that complete the famous proverb about determination.

terms.meaning: An adverb of place introducing a conditional or relative clause
terms.example: Where there is smoke, there is fire.
terms.connects: opens the conditional clause of the proverb

terms.meaning: An expletive 'there' used to introduce the existence of something (there is/are...)
terms.example: There is a solution to every problem.
terms.connects: fills both blanks: 'there is a will' and 'there is a way'

terms.meaning: Possessive pronoun — belonging to them; does not fit the proverb structure
terms.example: Their effort paid off.
terms.connects: distracting homophone to be eliminated

given.plan: Recognise the proverb 'Where there is a will, there is a way' and match each word to the correct blank.

steps.title: Recall the proverb
steps.apply: The well-known English proverb is 'Where there is a will, there is a way.' The two blanks map directly: blank 1 = 'Where', blank 2 = 'there'. The sentence given has the structure: '___ is a will, ___ is a way', which is an abbreviated form with the expletive 'there' omitted from the puzzle — but the only sensible completion is the classic proverb.
steps.note: Recognising the proverb instantly gives the answer without any analysis.

steps.title: Eliminate distractors
steps.apply: Options with 'Were' (past tense) or 'their' (possessive pronoun) cannot complete this adverbial proverb structure. Only 'Where' + 'there' (option C) makes grammatical and semantic sense.
steps.note: 'Were' and 'where' are often confused due to similar spelling; context and meaning distinguish them.

questions.formula_note: Proverb recognition: 'Where there is a will, there is a way' — 'where' is an adverb of place and 'there' is an expletive, not a possessive or a past verb form.

questions.question:
Despite the new medicine's _________ in treating diabetes, it is not _________ widely.

A. effectiveness — prescribed  B. availability — used  C. prescription — available  D. acceptance — proscribed
questions.to_find: the pair of words that correctly fills both blanks to make a coherent sentence about the new medicine

understand.plain: The sentence describes a contrast: the medicine has some positive quality in treating diabetes, yet it is not widely distributed/used. The first blank needs a positive attribute (like effectiveness) and the second blank needs a word meaning 'made available or used by doctors/patients' (like prescribed). Option A — 'effectiveness' and 'prescribed' — creates this logical contrast perfectly.

keywords.explain: The degree to which something succeeds in producing the desired result.
keywords.example: The effectiveness of the vaccine was demonstrated in large trials.

keywords.explain: Officially recommended by a medical professional for a patient.
keywords.example: The antibiotic was prescribed to treat the infection.

keywords.explain: Forbidden or officially prohibited — a near-homophone of 'prescribed' but opposite in meaning.
keywords.example: The drug was proscribed after dangerous side effects were found.

understand.visual_alt: A two-column table: Blank 1 requires a positive medical attribute (effectiveness); Blank 2 requires a word for widespread medical use (prescribed).

given.aim: Select the word pair that fills both blanks logically, creating a coherent sentence about a medicine's merit versus its limited use.

terms.meaning: Without being affected by; in spite of — signals a contrast between the two clauses
terms.example: Despite the rain, the match continued.
terms.connects: signals that the first blank (positive quality) contrasts with the second blank (limited use)

terms.meaning: A noun describing a desirable property the medicine has in treating diabetes
terms.example: effectiveness, efficacy, potency
terms.connects: must be a positive medical trait

terms.meaning: A word meaning used or distributed medically, but here negated with 'not... widely'
terms.example: prescribed, administered, dispensed
terms.connects: must indicate the medicine is not widely in use despite its merit

given.plan: Use the contrast set up by 'despite' to filter for a positive first word and a medically appropriate second word; only option A satisfies both.

steps.title: Apply the contrast logic of 'despite'
steps.apply: 'Despite X, it is not Y widely' requires X to be positive (a merit) and Y to mean 'used in medical practice'. Option A: 'effectiveness' (positive merit) and 'prescribed' (standard medical distribution term) — correct contrast. Option B: 'availability' could work as first blank but 'used' is too vague. Option C: 'prescription' in the first blank sounds like the medicine exists as a prescription drug, not a positive quality. Option D: 'proscribed' means forbidden, contradicting 'not proscribed widely' (which would mean it IS widely available).
steps.note: Watch out for D: 'proscribed' (forbidden) vs 'prescribed' (recommended by a doctor) — they are near-homophones with opposite meanings.

steps.title: Verify option A
steps.apply: 'Despite the new medicine's effectiveness in treating diabetes, it is not prescribed widely.' This reads naturally: the medicine works well, but doctors do not prescribe it often. The logic is clear and the vocabulary is precise.
steps.note: In GATE GA, always verify by reading the completed sentence aloud to check naturalness.

questions.formula_note: Contextual vocabulary: 'despite' sets up a contrast requiring a positive attribute in the first blank and a word for medical distribution in the second; only 'effectiveness — prescribed' satisfies both conditions.
